participants.reasoning: The extracted participant description matches the official criteria, recruitment, sample size, and dropout information without any substantive contradictions.
participants.extracted: Seventy caregivers of outpatients with schizophrenia were recruited from records available at three psychiatric centers in Shiraz, Iran. Patients had lived with their families for at least two years, had a DSM-IV diagnosis of schizophrenia within the preceding five years, had no other Axis I disorder at recruitment, and were not abusing substances. Each participating caregiver identified as the primary family caregiver, had no known mental illness, and had not taken part in another psychoeducational program during the previous year. Caregivers were randomly allocated to an experimental group (n=35) or a control group (n=35); after dropouts, 65 families completed the study (33 experimental, 32 control).

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention on format, duration, session count, content, delivery, and control condition, with only minor differences in wording and additional detail.
interventions.extracted: The experimental group received a family psycho-educational program for caregivers, delivered in four small groups of 8–9 caregivers each. The intervention consisted of 10 sessions, each lasting 90 minutes, held over 5 weeks (two sessions per week) in the afternoon at a time chosen for convenience. The program was led by a psychiatric nurse or psychiatrist and was based on an initial needs assessment of the families. Sessions used lectures, visual aids (charts, films, PowerPoint slides), group discussion, and question-and-answer periods; a clinically stable patient with schizophrenia also spoke in one session to share personal experiences. The content covered orientation to the program and assessment of family needs, understanding schizophrenia and its symptoms, etiology and treatment, coping with problematic behaviors and symptoms, communication and problem-solving skills, relapse prevention, medication adherence and supervision, stress management, and family support. An educational booklet was provided at the end of the program. The control group received routine care only, consisting of antipsychotic drug treatment for the patients without the caregiver psychoeducation intervention.
interventions.official:
Group 1: family psychoeducation (n = 35)
Content: the caregivers participated in a psycho‐educational programme while the patients received antipsychotic drug treatment, including assessment of family needs; to understand schizophrenia; medications and compliance; warning signs of relapse and relapse prevention; communication skills in the family; to manage the patient's symptom and skills in coping with them; effective way to express emotion; stress management in the family; relaxation methods

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes match the official report for psychiatric symptoms (BPRS) and caregiver burden (validated Persian family burden questionnaire), with the extra extracted medication-compliance detail not conflicting with the listed outcomes.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure patient psychiatric symptoms using the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS), including the global BPRS score and its symptom domains (positive symptoms, negative symptoms, mania-hostility symptoms, and depressive-anxiety symptoms), assessed at baseline (time 0), immediately after the intervention (time 1), and one month post-intervention (time 2). It also planned to measure family caregiver burden using the Family Burden questionnaire (Persian version), assessed at baseline, immediately after the intervention, and one month post-intervention. In addition, patient compliance to taking medication was collected as a participant characteristic/clinical variable using a specially designed questionnaire, with assessment timing not clearly specified in the report. No mortality, hospital admission, quality of life, stress, or adverse event measurement instruments were specified in the study description.
